revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jingū taima]][[ja:神宮大麻]][[zh:神宮大麻]]


{{Short description|Charm from Ise Jingu}}
{{italic title}}
{{Proofreader needed|神宮大麻|ja|date=August 2022}}
{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Jingu taima front.jpg|thumb|An example of ''Jingū taima'']]}}
{{nihongo|'''Jingū Taima'''|神宮大麻|Jingū Taima or Jingū Ōnusa{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}}}} is an ''{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa|1=Ōnusa}}''<ref name="haraegushi">{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉 |first=精選版 |title=祓串とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%93%E4%B8%B2-604891 |access-date=2022-05-05 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-01-01 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220101165700/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%93%E4%B8%B2-604891 |url-status=live }}</ref> wrapped in clean {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}} ''{{ill|Washi|en|Washi|qid=Q846299|lt=Washi|1=Washi}}'' and issued by the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. They are a form of ''[[ofuda]]''.<ref name="nakanishi" /> The [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] recommends every household have at least three Ofuda in their [[Kamidana]], a Jingu Taima, an [[Ujigami]] ofuda, and another deity one personally chooses.<ref name="jinjanohiroba" /><ref name="wagokoro" /><ref name="green-shinto-2011a" /><ref name="association-of-shinto-shrines-jinja-honcho-2020a" /><ref name="tokyo-jinjacho-2020a" />

== History ==
[[File:Jingu_taima_gaku.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Different types of {{transliteration|ja|Jingū Taima}} (upper row) and other talismans distributed in the Inner Shrine ({{transliteration|ja|[[Ise Grand Shrine#Naikū – the inner shrine|Naikū]]}}) of Ise. From left: {{nihongo||海幸大麻|Kaikō taima}} (a kind of {{transliteration|ja|kifuda}} for luck in fishing), {{nihongo||剣祓|kenharai}}, {{nihongo||角祓|kakuharai}}, and {{nihongo||大角祓|daikakuharai}} (a larger version of {{transliteration|ja|kakuharai}}).]]
''Jingu Taima'' were originally {{nihongo|{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=purification|1=Harae}} wands|祓串|haraegushi}} that wandering preachers associated with the shrines of {{nihongo|Ise|御師|oshi or onshi}} handed out to devotees across the country as a sign and guarantee that prayers were conducted on their behalf. These wands, called {{nihongo||御祓大麻|Oharai Taima}}, were contained either in packets of folded paper – in which case they are called {{nihongo||剣祓|kenharai}} (also {{transliteration|ja|kenbarai}}),<ref name="Breen">{{cite journal |last1=Breen |first1=John |date=2010 |title=Resurrecting the Sacred Land of Japan: The State of Shinto in the Twenty-First Century |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/download/digidepo_9592058_po_849.pdf?contentNo=1&alternativeNo= |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=Nanzan Institute for Religion and Culture |volume=37 |issue=2 |pages=295–315 |access-date=2022-05-05 |archive-date=2022-05-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220505073705/https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/download/digidepo_9592058_po_849.pdf?contentNo=1&alternativeNo= |url-status=live }}</ref> due to the packet's shape resembling a {{nihongo|sword blade|剣|ken}} – or in boxes called {{nihongo||御祓箱|oharaibako}}. The widespread distribution of {{transliteration|ja|Oharai Taima}} first began in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and reached its peak in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}: a document dating from 1777 ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|qid=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 6) indicates that eighty-nine to ninety percent of all households in the country at the time owned an Ise talisman.<ref name="Breen" /><ref name="nakanishi">{{cite web |last1=Nakanishi |first1=Masayuki |title=Jingū taima |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9634|access-date=2022-07-28 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220728201343/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9634|archive-date=2022-07-28|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=神宮大麻と神宮暦 (''Jingū taima to Jingu-reki'') |url=https://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/jingutaima/index.html |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Ise Jingu Official Website |language=ja |archive-date=2020-10-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201010065512/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/jingutaima/index.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=第１４章 神宮大麻・暦 |url=https://fukushima-jinjacho.or.jp/monoshiri/pc/section14.html |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Fukushima Jinjachō Official Website |publisher=Fukushima Jinjachō |language=ja |archive-date=2018-06-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180615124127/http://fukushima-jinjacho.or.jp/monoshiri/pc/section14.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

Originally, the taima (exorcism skewer) used in the exorcism prayer was wrapped in Japanese paper and delivered to the recipient as a proof that the goshi had performed the exorcism prayer.<ref name="伊勢文化舎・伊勢商工会議所編『検定お伊勢さん公式テキストブック』(2006),28頁">伊勢文化舎・伊勢商工会議所編『検定お伊勢さん公式テキストブック』(2006),28頁</ref> Eventually, [[Ise Grand Shrine#Pilgrimage at Ise|Pilgrimage to Ise]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 |title=伊勢講とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E8%AC%9B-30948 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506180423/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E8%AC%9B-30948 |url-status=live }}</ref> and other [[:en:Kosha (lecture group)|Kosha]] were organized and distributed. During the Goshi period, exorcism sticks were distributed either in a box or wrapped in a sword-shaped omochi.<ref name="伊勢志摩経済新聞-2012a">{{Cite news |date=2012-04-20 |title=「神宮大麻」のご神体となる木を切り始める祭り、かつては国民の9割が家に |work=[[伊勢志摩経済新聞]] |url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/1126/ |access-date=2012-09-20 |archive-date=2013-04-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130406230845/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/1126/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Medieval and Early modern periods ===
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi era|1=Muromachi period}}, the distribution of taima became widely practiced.{{Sfn|足立康|1926|p=8}} During this period, the Goshishi of Ise made the rounds of parishioners, and prayers such as the 10,000-times exorcism<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉 |first=精選版 |title=一万度祓とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E4%B8%87%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-433705 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506175904/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E4%B8%87%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-433705 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |title=万度祓とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%87%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-636759 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506175903/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%87%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-636759 |url-status=live }}</ref> were offered to the shogun and others, but generally the equivalent of the Edo period sword exorcism was distributed{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=22}} Various items were distributed along with the taima, which gradually settled on the Ise calendar.{{Sfn|足立康|1926|p=10}} In the Edo period, taima for purification included 10,000-degree purification, 5,000-degree purification, 1,000-degree purification, and sword purification, with sword purification distributed to the general public.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=22-24}} There also existed 10,000-degree Kagura taima, 10,000-degree 100-day taima, and 5,000-degree Kagura taima,.<ref>『瑞垣第193号 近世の御祓大麻篠澤家長野県佐久市岩村田の神蔵 金鑽俊樹』伊勢神宮神宮司庁発行 平成14th year 56頁</ref> O-harai-taima-harae-gu (御祓大麻祓具) was used to make o-harai-taima (御祓大麻) at the place where the priest went.<ref name="140ani">{{Cite news |date=2012-09-08 |title=神札：頒布140周年記念、神宮大麻の歴史など紹介 伊勢・徴古館で企画展 ／三重（三重版朝刊） |work=毎日新聞 |url=http://mainichi.jp/feature/news/20120908ddlk24040016000c.html |access-date=2012-09-16 |archive-url=https://megalodon.jp/2012-0913-1641-20/mainichi.jp/feature/news/20120908ddlk24040016000c.html |archive-date=2012-09-08}}</ref> In preparing the exorcisms, the priests performed other celebratory rites and prayer texts in addition to the Nakatomi exorcisms, and these deities were enshrined as Shinmeisho and other places.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=24-25}}

During the Muromachi period, taima became widely distributed. During this period, the master of Ise went around the Danka system, and prayers such as the 10,000 degree ritual were performed on the shogunate, but in general, the sword ritual of the Edo period. The equivalent of the sword was distributed. Various items were distributed along with taima, which gradually became established in the Ise calendar. In the Edo period, there were 10,000-degree rituals, 5,000-degree rituals, 1,000-degree rituals, and sword rituals, and sword rituals were generally distributed. There were also 10,000 degree kagura taima, 10,000 degree 100 day taima, and 5,000 degree kagura taima. Oharaita Imaharaegu was used to make taima at the place where the master went. These destinations were enshrined as Shinmeisho, etc., because they played other congratulatory words and prayer sentences in the preparation of the Oharae.

People rushed to Ise Jingu Shrine in record numbers every 60 years from 1650 to 1830, and there was a great deal of noise about money falling from the heavens and illnesses being cured{{Sfn|松平乘昌|2008|pp=92-95}} In February 1830, {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|en|Awa Province (Tokushima)|qid=Q907457|lt=Awa|1=Awa Province (Tokushima)}}, money fell from heaven, and by August, nearly five million worshippers had flocked to Ise Jingu Shrine.{{Sfn|松平乘昌|2008|pp=92-95}} {{ill|Matsumura Keibun|en|Matsumura Keibun|qid=Q11531427|lt=Matsumura Keibun|1=Matsumura Keibun}} painted "The descent of the taima from the Shrines", which is in the collection of [[Ise, Mie#History Museum|Jingu Chokokan Museum]].{{Sfn|松平乘昌|2008|pp=92-95}}

=== Modern Period ===
[[File:EeJaNaiKaScene.jpg|left|thumb|230x230px|The end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} witnessed the rise of public festivities and protests known as {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Ee ja nai ka|en|Ee ja nai ka|qid=Q5347024|lt=Ee ja nai ka|1=Ee ja nai ka}}}} triggered by reports of talismans raining from the sky.]]
In 1871, an imperial decree abolished the {{transliteration|ja|oshi}} and allotted the production and distribution of the amulets, now renamed ''Jingu Taima'', to the shrine's administrative offices.<ref name="nakanishi" /> It was around this time that the talisman's most widely known form – a wooden tablet containing a sliver of {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|qid=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}} wood known as {{nihongo3|"sacred core"|御真|gyoshin}}<ref name="Breen" /><ref>{{cite web |title=三重）伊勢神宮で「大麻用材伐始祭」 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN4H6SBMN4HONFB002.html |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Asahi Shimbun Digital |date=15 April 2020 |archive-date=2020-04-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200427222631/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN4H6SBMN4HONFB002.html |url-status=live }}</ref> wrapped in {{ill|Ise washi|en|Ise washi|qid=Q11378970|lt=paper|1=Ise washi}} on which is printed the shrine's name (天照皇大神宮, ''Amaterasu-ōmikami-jingū'') and stamped with the seals of the shrine (皇大神宮御璽, ''Kōtai Jingū Gyoji'') and its high priest (大神宮司之印, ''Daijingūji no In'') – developed. In 1900, a new department, the {{nihongo3|"Department of Priests"|神部署|Kanbesho}}, took over production and distribution duties. The distribution of ''Jingu Taima'' was eventually delegated to the {{nihongo|{{ill|National Association of Shinto Priests|en|National Association of Shinto Priests|qid=Q11389505|lt=National Association of Shinto Priests|1=National Association of Shinto Priests}}|[[:ja:全国神職会|全国神職会]]|Zenkoku Shinshokukai}} in 1927 and finally to its successor, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.<ref name="nakanishi" /> The Association nowadays continues to disseminate ''Jingu Taima'' to affiliated shrines throughout Japan, where they are made available alongside the shrines' own amulets.

According to the explanation of [[Ise Grand Shrine]] Guji (chief priest) [[Jingūkyō|Urada Nagatami]] in 1873, it was the distribution of {{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa|1=Ōnusa}}, a sacred object to ward off sin.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=34}}

After the Meiji Era (1868-1912), it was transformed into a Shinto taima with the {{ill|Privy Seal of Japan|en|Privy Seal of Japan|qid=Q2915131|lt=Privy Seal of Japan|1=Privy Seal of Japan}} stamped on it. Since then, it has been wrapped as Goshin, the sacred body of the shrine.<ref name="伊勢志摩経済新聞-2012a" /> In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the system of goji was suspended with the formation of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and distributed throughout the country from the [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]] organized by the Ise Jingu from Ise-ko. From the Meiji era to the present, all Jingu taima has been consistently produced within Ise Jingu, and is distributed by the Jingu-kyo, then by the Jingu Bohoninkai Foundation, and later by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].

According to the 1916 explanation by the Jingu Shinbusho, it originated in {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}, an event that is now a prayer for the nation's people, and is therefore not a [[shintai]] or [[Bunrei|wakemitamama]], but rather a mark of veneration.{{Sfn|当山春三|1916|pp=5-22}} These arguments attempted to encompass the various beliefs of the time, but it was also an exorcism tool because it was offered as an offering to the gods, the spirit was attached to it and it became a sacred object, and it was also a prayer for purification.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}} It is said that we should not choose one over the other.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}}

== Varieties and usage ==
{{transliteration|ja|Shinsatsu}} such as ''Jingu Taima'' are enshrined in a household altar ({{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}) or a special stand ({{transliteration|ja|ofudatate}}); in the absence of one, they may be placed upright in a clean and tidy space above eye level or attached to a wall. {{transliteration|ja|Shinsatsu}} and the {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} that house them are set up facing east (where the sun rises), south (the principal direction of sunshine), or southeast.<ref name="jinjanohiroba">{{cite web |title=神棚のまつり方 |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/faq/faq_09.html |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Jinja no Hiroba |archive-date=2020-04-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200428095730/http://www.jinja.ne.jp/faq/faq_09.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="wagokoro">{{cite web |title=Household-shrine |url=https://en.wa-gokoro.jp/traditional-culture/kamidana/ |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Wagokoro |archive-date=2020-07-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200715152747/https://en.wa-gokoro.jp/traditional-culture/kamidana/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="shiroyama">{{cite web |title=御神札・御守・撤饌等の扱い方について |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/jinseigirei/tessen.html |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=城山八幡宮 (Shiroyama Hachiman-gū) |language=ja |archive-date=2019-04-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190407033335/http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/jinseigirei/tessen.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=お守りやお札の取り扱い |url=http://www.hyogomirai.com/life/column/content.php?cid=000017 |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=ja兵庫みらい (JA Hyōgo Mirai) |language=ja |archive-date=2022-07-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220729220115/https://www.hyogomirai.com/life/column/content.php?cid=000017 |url-status=live }}</ref>

The Association of Shinto Shrines recommends that a household own at least three kinds of {{transliteration|ja|shinsatsu}}:

# ''Jingu Taima''
# The {{transliteration|ja|ofuda}} of the tutelary deity of one's place of residence ({{transliteration|ja|[[ujigami]]}})
# The {{transliteration|ja|ofuda}} of a shrine one is personally devoted to {{nihongo||崇敬神社|sūkei jinja}}

[[File:Ofuda_Arrangement.png|left|thumb|Various possible ways of arranging {{transliteration|ja|ofuda}} ({{transliteration|ja|shinsatsu}}) in a Shinto altar]]
In a {{nihongo|'three-door' style|三社造|sansha-zukuri}} altar, the ''Jingu Taima'' is placed in the middle, with the {{transliteration|ja|ofuda}} of one's local {{transliteration|ja|ujigami}} on its left (observer's right) and the {{transliteration|ja|ofuda}} of one's favourite shrine on its right (observer's left). Alternatively, in a {{nihongo|'one-door' style|一社造|issha-zukuri}} {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}, the three talismans are laid on top of one another, with the ''Jingu Taima'' on the front. One may own more {{transliteration|ja|shinsatsu}}; these are placed on either side of or behind the aforementioned three.<ref name="jinjanohiroba" /><ref name="wagokoro" /><ref name="green-shinto-2011a">{{cite web |title=Ofuda (talisman) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2011/07/30/ofuda/ |access-date=2020-05-24 |website=Green Shinto |date=30 July 2011 |archive-date=2020-05-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200529111307/http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2011/07/30/ofuda/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="association-of-shinto-shrines-jinja-honcho-2020a">{{cite web |title=お神札（ふだ）のまつり方 (Ofuda no matsurikata) |url=https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/omatsuri/katei/ofuda |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Association of Shinto Shrines (Association of Shinto Shrines) |archive-date=2020-05-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200521134835/https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/omatsuri/katei/ofuda |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="tokyo-jinjacho-2020a">{{cite web |title=お神札・神棚について |url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ofuda_kamidana/ |access-date=2020-05-23 |website=Tokyo Jinjachō |language=ja |archive-date=2020-04-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200427141103/http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ofuda_kamidana/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Regular (preferably daily) worship before the {{transliteration|ja|shinsatsu}} or {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} and offerings of rice, salt, water, and/or {{transliteration|ja|[[sake]]}} to the {{transliteration|ja|kami}} (with additional foodstuffs being offered on special occasions) are recommended.<ref name="wagokoro" /><ref>{{cite book |last1=Toyozaki |first1=Yōko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SsmN4jxwnfUC&pg=PA61 |title=「日本の衣食住」まるごと事典 (Who Invented Natto?) |date=2007 |publisher=IBC Publishing |isbn=9784896846409 |pages=59–61 |access-date=2022-05-05 |archive-date=2022-05-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220505073704/https://books.google.com/books?id=SsmN4jxwnfUC&pg=PA61 |url-status=live }}</ref> The manner of worship is similar to those performed in shrines: two bows, two claps, and a final bow, though a prayer ({{transliteration|ja|[[norito]]}}) - also preceded by two bows - may be recited before this.<ref>{{cite web |title=神棚と神拝作法について教えて下さい。 |url=http://musashimitakejinja.jp/2017/12/09/question5/ |access-date=2020-05-24 |website=武蔵御嶽神社 (Musashi Mitake Jinja) |language=ja |archive-date=2020-09-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926000101/http://musashimitakejinja.jp/2017/12/09/question5/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=神棚の祀り方と参拝方法 |url=http://www.kumanosakaki.com/sanpai.html |access-date=2020-05-24 |website=熊野ワールド【神々の宿る熊野の榊】 |language=ja |archive-date=2019-10-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191029123810/http://www.kumanosakaki.com/sanpai.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Onshi ==
Although there have been examples of purification ceremonies performed since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} and {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura period}} periods, the earliest known example of the word "purification" is said to be a prayer performed by Minamoto no Yoritomo, described in {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} (Azuma Mirror), August 16, 1180,<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=一千度祓とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E5%8D%83%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-2007364 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506175725/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E5%8D%83%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-2007364 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |title=千度祓とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8D%83%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-88752 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506175726/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8D%83%E5%BA%A6%E7%A5%93-88752 |url-status=live }}</ref> in which he performed "1,000 times purification{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=18}}"

The exorcism is given to the recipient after the prayer.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=18}} [[Ōharae no Kotoba|Nakatomi-no-harae]] was recited, and this became the name of the sacred card.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=20-21}}

At that time, when the common people wanted to worship Ise Jingu, they had no choice but to perform purification through an oshi (priest) and play kagura (Shinto music and dance).{{Sfn|足立康|1926|p=8}}

As the activities of the Goshi grew, he dispatched deputies to the provinces.{{Sfn|足立康|1926|p=9}}

== Purification skewer==
Generally, the "Ohanusa" used for purification consists of a sakaki with {{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|qid=Q2657982|lt=Shide|1=Shide (Shinto)}} and ramie,<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,動植物名よみかた辞典 普及版,デジタル大辞泉 |first=精選版 |title=麻苧とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BA%BB%E8%8B%A7-423965 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506173319/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BA%BB%E8%8B%A7-423965 |url-status=live }}</ref> or only with shidare attached, and at Ise Shrine, only with ramie attached.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=安江和伸 |date=April 1992 |title=大麻（おおぬさ）考 |journal=神道史研究 |volume=40 |issue=2 |pages=74–99}}</ref>

Although the ancient sword-purification type of Jingu Omasha is also distributed at Ise Jingu Shrine, those distributed at various shrines throughout Japan are in the tag form. In kaku exorcism, it is wrapped in hemp ramie (string-like fiber).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=村田陽子 |author-link=村田陽子 |date=2002 |title=三重県の麻について |journal=民俗服飾研究論集 |issue=16 |pages=1–13}}</ref>

=== The eight-needle skewer ===
The priest would set up a skewer tied to a plan called a hakusokuan (eight foot plan), which was used during dosubarae (purification by degrees).<ref name="國學院20">{{Cite web |title=資料解説 » 20 祓具 |url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/kaihatsu/oharai/m_20.html |access-date=2012-09-24 |publisher=國學院大學伝統文化リサーチセンター |archive-date=2016-03-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306013901/http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/kaihatsu/oharai/m_20.html |url-status=live }}</ref> At the back of the top of the plan, a piece of paper for money and a piece of sugama<ref name="日本国語大辞典">{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=菅麻とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8F%85%E9%BA%BB-2052972 |access-date=2022-05-06 |website=コトバンク |language=ja |archive-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220506180029/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%8F%85%E9%BA%BB-2052972 |url-status=live }}</ref> the eight-hari skewer with a piece of paper and a piece of Suga hemp is set up at the back of the top of the plan.{{Sfn|松平乘昌|2008|p=98}} The eight-foot plan can fit in a box and is portable.<ref name="國學院20" /> This is called yakura-oki shinji, which is similar to the annual festival of the Ise Jingu shrine.<ref>{{Cite web |title= Gr.jp|url=http://www.mkc.gr.jp/seitoku/pdf/f25-4.pdf |access-date=2022-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171017151025/http://www.mkc.gr.jp/seitoku/pdf/f25-4.pdf |archive-date=2017-10-17 }}</ref>

This was a closely guarded secret that was not to be taken west of the Miyagawa River, but in 1852 it was discovered at Isedono Shrine in Saitama Prefecture.{{Sfn|松平乘昌|2008|p=98}}

=== Hashiri-shushi ===
The goji (priest) set up a bound purification skewer called an yashigushi (still gushi) on a plan called a hakusokuan, which was used during dosuubarae.<ref name="Kokugakuin 20">{{Cite web |title=Documentary Description " 20 Exorcism Skewer |url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/kaihatsu/oharai/m_20.html |access-date=2012-09-24 |publisher=Kokugakuin University Research Center for Traditional Culture |archive-date=2016-03-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306013901/http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/kaihatsu/oharai/m_20.html |url-status=live }}</ref> An eight-foot-long skewer with a piece of paper for Shinto paper and Sugamasa<ref name="日本国語大辞典"/> is set up at the back on top of the draft.{{CN|date=September 2023}} The Hachisoku Draft can be stored in a box and is portable.<ref name="Kokugakuin 20" /> This is called yakura-oki shinji, which is similar to the annual festival of the Ise Jingu shrine.<ref>[http://www.mkc.gr.jp/seitoku/pdf/f25-4.pdf Archived copy] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171017151025/http://www.mkc.gr.jp/seitoku/pdf/f25-4.pdf |date=2017-10-17 }}</ref>

This was a closely guarded secret not to be taken west of the Miyagawa River, but in 1852 it was discovered at the Isedono Shrine in Saitama Prefecture.{{CN|date=September 2023}}

=== Jingu Omamasa no Mikoto ===

The Goshin in the Jingu Daima is also called {{Nihongo|Exorcism skewer|祓串|Haraigushi}}{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=130-133}} or {{Nihongo|Hemp skewer|麻串|Asakushi}}<ref>{{Cite news |date=2003-04-17 |title=「大麻用材伐始式」粛々と 麻串に加工、全国へ配布 伊勢神宮／三重 |page=18 |work=毎日新聞 |location=地方版／三重}}</ref>

=== Mamori-Harai ===
[[File:Mamori_harai_of_Ise_Grand_Shrine.jpg|thumb|210x210px|Mamori-Harai at the Inner Shrine of Ise Jingu.]]
{{Nihongo|Mamori-Harai|守祓|まもりはらい}} is a small sacred card inside an amulet.<ref>[http://www.isejingu.or.jp/visit/amulet/geku.html 内宮の授与品] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220615023908/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/visit/amulet/geku.html |date=2022-06-15 }} （伊勢神宮） 2017th year 10月18日閲覧。</ref> Only Mamori-Harai for each shrine in Ise can be purchased and put together in one amulet bag.<ref>{{Cite web |last=しらべぇ |date=2015-12-10 |title=地元民が教える「伊勢神宮」最強パワスポ3選 |url=http://top.tsite.jp/news/buzz/o/26745431/index |access-date=2017-10-10 |publisher=T-SITE }}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

== The Imperial Seal and taima ==

=== Cessation and Reform of the Goshishi System ===
In the Meiji era (1868-1912), the activities of the Goshishi ceased in the process of establishing {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}.

The Imperial Grand Shrine at Ise, Oharai-cho, just before the Uji Bridge, was once the site of the Goshishi's residence.{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=136}}

On December 18, 1871, the {{ill|High priest|en|High priest|qid=Q3114821|lt=High priest|1=High priest}} of Ise Jingu [[:en:Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]] requested that taima be stamped with the divine name of "Amaterasu" and "Imperial Seal of the Imperial Grand Shrine" and distributed universally under the responsibility of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ministry of Divinities]], which the ministry approved.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=32-34}}
In January of the following year, the taima Manufacturing Bureau was established, and in April, the taima distribution rules were established.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=32-34}} On April 1, the "Privy Seal of Japan" was held, and Omiya Tsukasa's Kitakoji Zumitsu played the distribution of taima as a privy seal.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=32-34}}

In the "Jingu Omasho Shiki" (Jingu Shrine Omasha Enshrinement Ceremony) of December 1873 (Meiji 6), Nagatami Urada, a minor shrine priest, stated that people should worship every morning and evening in order to eliminate sins and impurities by facing and worshipping Omasha, a sacred object distributed annually from the shrine to ward off sins.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=34}}

On December 18, 1871 (Meiji 4), the priest of Ise Jingu Omiya stamped the name of the taima on the [[:en:Ministry of Shinto|Shinto ministry]] as "Tensho Emperor Daijingu" and "Ise Grand Shrine Privy" at the responsibility of the [[:en:Jingu Shrine|Jingu Shrine]]. The Ministry of Divinities approved this, asking for a uniform distribution. In January of the following year, the taima Manufacturing Bureau was established, and in April, the taima distribution rules were established. On April 1, the "Privy Seal of Japan" was held, and Omiya Tsukasa Kitakoji Zumitsu played the distribution of taima as a large privy seal. At the "Jingu Taima Dedication Ceremony" in December 1873 (Meiji 6), Chomin Urata, the priest of the shrine, worships at the taima as a sacred tool that dispels the sins distributed from the shrine every year. It states that we should worship every morning and evening in order to exhaust the impurities.

=== Change of distributor ===
[[File:皇大神宮古札納所.jpg|thumb|Ancient card storage near the entrance to the [[Ise Grand Shrine#Naikū – the inner shrine|Imperial Grand Shrine]] at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]]]The Jingu-kyoin was opened in 1873, and Article 5 of the Jingu-kyoin Regulations of February 1876 indicates that the distribution of Jingu-taima is its main business.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=194-195}}

After the notification of 1878 (Meiji 11), the method of distribution was changed frequently.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=35-36}}

There is also a large, thick, and thickly presented taima in a wooden box that is presented to the imperial family and former emperors.<ref>
{{Cite news |last=中村尚徳 |author-link=中村尚徳 |date=2012-09-12 |title=「神札と伊勢信仰」の企画展 |work=朝日新聞 |url=http://mytown.asahi.com/mie/news.php?k_id=25000001209120006 |access-date=2012-09-16 |archive-url=https://megalodon.jp/2012-0915-2344-10/mytown.asahi.com/mie/news.php?k_id=25000001209120006 |archive-date=2012-09-15}}</ref>

In 1882, when the Jingu-kyoin became independent as the Shinto Jingu-ha and further became the Jingu-kyo, the distribution of taima was entrusted to the Jingu-kyo.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=35-36}} In 1898 (Meiji 31), Jingu-kyo became Jingu Bonshinkai, and distribution was entrusted to this organization.{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|pp=35-36}}

Article 21 of the 1882 Shinto Shrine Office Kyōin Classification and Article 7(7) of the 1899 Ministry of the Interior Instruction No. 823 legally prohibited these conflations because the distributed taima has a portion that prays for the emperor or imperial family, but the conferred taima does not.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}}

The distributing taima blesses the dedicator under the Imperial Guard.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}} According to Article 21 of the Jingu Shrine Academy Division in 1882 and Article 7 (7) of the Ministry of Interior Lesson No. 823 of 1899 (Meiji 32), the distributed taima has a part to pray for the emperor and the imperial family. Since there is no such confusion, these confusions are legally prohibited. Distribution taima protects stoners under the patronage of the empire.

The significance of the freedom of worship was advocated, and it was explained by the Jingu Shrine department in 1916 (Taisho 5), but the origin of Jingu taima is {{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Onusa|1=Ōnusa}}. It was {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}} by [[Norito]], and now it is an event that prays to the national people, so it is a Shintai or a spirit. It is not, therefore, not something like [[Honzon ]], but a mark for worship, so it does not conflict with religious freedom.{{Sfn|当山春三|1916|pp=5-22}}

However, the congratulatory rites of the early 21st century, even those distributed by the ministry, read, "We have dedicated the Taima, so we will offer it before the gods and hold a purification ceremony, so let the spirits of the dead come to this Taima.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}} The theory from the Taisho era to the prewar Showa era denies the existence of the Spirit, and says that it is a symbol of far-off worship. However, there are various theories, such as mihakubaku, exorcism tools, kintai, and symbols, which attempt to include various beliefs of the time.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}} It is said that one should not choose any of them.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}}

In 2003, the "Jingu Taima Historical Materials Mysterious Revised First Edition" of the Jingu Shrine Education Division was published, and the following year, the "Study Group Report on Jingu Taima" of the Association of Shinto Shrine Education Division was published.{{Sfn|大野由之|2014}} The number of taima distributed in 2011 was 8,885,545.<ref name="140ani"/>

== See also ==

== Bibliography ==

* {{Cite book |last=[[寺本慧達]]編 |url={{NDLDC|1094201}} |title=神宮大麻調査文献 |publisher=東林書房 |year=1930 }}
* {{Cite book |last=当山春三 |url={{NDLDC|925273}} |title=神宮大麻と国民性 |publisher=神宮奉斎会本部 |year=1916 |author-link=当山春三}}
* {{Cite book |last=西川順土 |title=近代の神宮 |publisher=神宮司庁 |year=1988 |series=神宮教養叢書第9 |author-link=西川順土}}
* {{Cite book |last=三好和義 |title=伊勢神宮 |publisher=淡交社 |others=[[岡野弘彦]]ほか |year=2003 |isbn=978-4473031082 |series=日本の古社 |author-link=三好和義}}

* 崇敬会だよりみもすそ40号-特集神宮大麻をご家庭に（伊勢神宮崇敬会） - ウェイバックマシン（2016th year 3月14日アーカイブ分）
* [https://jpsearch.go.jp/csearch/jps-cross?csid=jps-cross&from=0&size=100&keyword=%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E9%BA%BB 横断検索 神宮大麻] - ジャパンサーチ

<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->



[[en:Jingū taima]]

<!-- unsure if this is the right citation as it came from the sentence after originally --><!-- 御師 --><!-- 祓串 --><!-- original text これは八座置神事（やくらおきしんじ）と呼ばれ伊勢神宮の恒例祭にも似たものが見られる。 --><!-- 神宮大麻の御真 --><!-- original text 神宮大麻の中の御真は祓串[65]または麻串とも呼ばれる[66]。
 --><!--
Don't know what Omasha is

1873rd year （明治6th year ）12月の『神宮大麻奉祀式』において、少宮司の浦田長民が、毎年神宮から頒布する罪を払除けする神具としての大麻に向かい敬拝することで、罪穢れを消尽するがために、毎朝夕礼拝すべし、というようなことを述べている{{Sfn|西川順土|1988|p=34}}。--><!--== Festivals ==

=== 大麻修祓式 ===
大麻修祓式（たいましゅはつしき）は随時行われる{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|p=114}}。 The taima stoner ceremony is held at any time.

=== 大麻暦奉製始祭 ===
1月8日に、大麻暦奉製始祭（たいまれきほうせいはじめさい）が行われる{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=130-133}}。On January 8th, the taima calendar dedication festival (Taimare Kihosei Hajimesai) is held.

=== 大麻暦頒布終了祭 ===
3月1日に、大麻暦頒布終了祭が皇大神宮神楽殿にて行われる{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=130-133}}。On March 1st, a taima calendar distribution end festival will be held at the Imperial Grand Shrine Kaguraden.

=== 大麻用材伐始祭 ===
4月に、大麻用材伐始祭（たいまようざいきりはじめさい）が行われ、神宮林より切り出された用材は、長さ20センチメートル、厚さ1ミリメートルに加工され{{ill|Ise washi|en|Ise washi|qid=Q11378970|lt=和紙|1=Ise washi}}を巻き付け、神宮大麻の中心の御真（ぎょしん）に用いられる<ref>{{Cite news |date=2012-04-20 |title=伊勢　用材切り出しの安全祈願　内宮前で大麻用材伐始祭 |work=伊勢新聞 |url=http://www.isenp.co.jp/news/20120420/news04.htm |access-date=2012-09-16}}</ref>。In April, the taima timber cutting festival was held, and the timber cut out from the Jingu forest was processed to a length of 20 cm and a thickness of 1 mm, wrapped with Japanese paper , and Jingu taima. Used for the central taima.

=== 大麻暦頒布始祭 ===
9月17日に、大麻暦頒布始祭（たいまれきはんぷはじめさい）が皇大神宮神楽殿にて行われる{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=130-133}}。On September 17, the taima Calendar Distribution Beginning Festival will be held at the Imperial Grand Shrine Kaguraden.

=== 大麻暦奉製終了祭 ===
12月20日に、大麻暦奉製終了祭が行われる{{Sfn|三好和義|2003|pp=130-133}}。On December 20, the taima calendar dedication end festival will be held.

=== taima stoner ceremony [ edit source ] ===
The taima stoner ceremony is held at any time.

=== taima calendar stoner festival [ edit source ] ===
On January 8th, the taima calendar dedication festival (Taimare Kihosei Hajimesai) is held.

=== taima calendar distribution end festival [ edit source ] ===
On March 1st, a taima calendar distribution end festival will be held at the Imperial Grand Shrine Kaguraden.

=== taima Timber Cutting Festival [ Edit Source ] ===
In April, the taima timber cutting festival was held, and the timber cut out from the Jingu forest was processed to a length of 20 cm and a thickness of 1 mm, wrapped with Japanese paper , and Jingu taima. Used for the central taima.

=== taima calendar distribution start festival [ edit source ] ===
On September 17, the taima Calendar Distribution Beginning Festival will be held at the Imperial Grand Shrine Kaguraden.

=== taima calendar dedication end festival [ edit source ] ===
On December 20, the taima calendar dedication end festival will be held.
-->
